items.author: mrgretzky

items.content:
There are a lot of static analyzers, sanitizers, debuggers and so on.

If you are going to use a LLM, you will have to force it to be extra careful. The LLM is designed to be rewarded by work being done fast, and that's why you cannot trust them alone, I personally think.
items.author: zuhaitz_dev

items.content:
If not, what you will get is a lot of code full of vulnerabilities, and you can end up putting people at risk.

My point is, perhaps you don't need to know everything under the hood although it would be optimal, but if you don't, at least use all of the tools available.
items.author: zuhaitz_dev

items.content:
You will always make a better work by writing the best machine code than by writing the best compiled code (with the compilers we have).

LLMs can be useful too, like compilers, but a lot of people who use them don't take rigorous steps to assure quality. That's why slop exists.

items.author: 0gtweet

items.content:
DFIR/persistence challenge, anyone?

I've published the first two challenges. Difficulty level: easy. More are coming, and they’ll get harder - trust me!

Run a command and observe malware.txt reappearing on the desktop after every restart. Your task is to find and eliminate the root cause.

quick test: 'test' | % { iwr "https://t.co/1KL2vh9HBe" -UseBasicParsing} | iex
first challenge: 'challenge01' | % { iwr "https://t.co/1KL2vh9HBe" -UseBasicParsing} | iex
second challenge: 'challenge02' | % { iwr "https://t.co/1KL2vh9HBe" -UseBasicParsing} | iex

Fine print:
1. It's not directly harmful but never run it in production.
2. If you reply to this post, please mark your answers as spoilers so you don't ruin the fun for others.
3. Tested on a freshly installed Windows Server 2025 Enterprise with Desktop Experience VM.
4. Feel free to reach out if you'd like to add own challenges!

items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
RT @AretiqAI: New Research: CVE-2026-45502 — Microsoft Exchange Server SSRF

Any mailbox user can force Exchange to make HTTP requests to internal networks. The SSRF protection only runs on cloud deployments — on-premises servers skip the check entirely.

Root cause: the intranet address validation is gated on `isBposUser`, which is always `false` for on-prem Exchange. One SOAP request to EWS InstallApp with a crafted ManifestUrl = blind SSRF from the Exchange server's network position.

Affects Exchange 2016 CU23, 2019 CU14/CU15, and Exchange SE. Patched in the June 2026 SU.

Full analysis + PoC: https://t.co/jiEtZmEQsZ

items.author: Salsa12__

items.content:
RT @XorNinja: We pulled in $117,000 in Chrome bug bounties with simple tricks; on Wednesday, Quang Luong will spill his secrets at the Stanford AI Security Conference: 
https://t.co/Fhq0NH13jn

Fun fact: Quang is probably the only researcher in the known universe who still uses Gemini to find bugs.

Before the end of the year, Calif researchers will be presenting at Blackhat USA, Defcon, and Hexacon. We're also hoping to make it to Unprompted AU, OffensiveCon, and Objective By The Sea.

At Black Hat USA, Dionysus Blazakis and the team will walk through the bugs and exploit chain used in the Apple MIE bypass discovered a few months ago.
https://t.co/dfeYJSzFVT

At DEF CON, we will tell the story of hacking software that helps run the Internet backbone.

At Hexacon in Paris, @brucedang  and I will give the keynote. Apple announced MIE there last year, so it'll be a fun one. I suspect they only wanted Bruce, but keynotes require a certain amount of professional nonsense, and Bruce is far too honest for that, so I got invited too. My job is marketing, which is to lie without getting caught.

What's wild is that none of this existed at the beginning of the year.

We started with a simple realization: very few people have both deep security expertise and access to the best AI models.

So we went all in and never looked back.

Back in March, we called a company-wide all-hands on a Saturday. The title of the invite was: "AI Tsunami and Our Actions."

I don't want to romanticize overwork, but what we were seeing felt too urgent to wait until Monday.

Then everyone started cooking. The results have been spectacular.

Our research on defeating Apple MIE made it into The Wall Street Journal. We signed major contracts with Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and xAI. While others are celebrating access to the latest models, we've been using them to explore the frontiers of vulnerability research.

In the first half of 2026, we're already surpassing our entire 2025 bookings. Most importantly, we've assembled a top-tier team in record time.

I've read many strategy books, but this is the first time I've witnessed the power of the right strategy at the right time.

Focus is the name of the game. Strategy is deciding what to ignore. For one month and a half, we stopped starting new projects. I've personally shelved a lifelong passion in Vietnam, because it isn't a priority for the company. You can only move fast when you're light.

Several people were upset when we changed direction so abruptly. That's normal. If nobody complains, you probably didn't focus.

Of course, strategy isn't magic. You can make a focused bet and still be wrong. We were fortunate that this one worked out.

None of this would be possible without our partners and supporters across the frontier labs. Thank you.

items.content:
been building CogniFind, a local semantic search for files on windows. 
onnx embeddings with sqlite-vec, processed entirely on device. 
it works with the network off. nothing about your files is ever sent anywhere.
it indexes pdf, word, excel and markdown in the background and lets you search by meaning instead of filenames. 
if you're like me and can never remember what was in which file, it might help a bit.😇

repo: https://t.co/hX4ItPfPz7

items.author: kyleavery

items.content:
RT @nickvangilder: The uncomfortable truth about leveling up in offsec is that it may not be your tradecraft that’s holding you back. Learn the tradecraft though. Command and control. Evasion primitives. Direct syscalls, reflective loading. AMSI patching. All that stuff matters and you should definitely put in the reps.

That said, some of the best operators I know spend a huge chunk of their time understanding the tech itself, the way an engineer who built it would. Not the exploit for the tech, the plumbing. How does this Okta auth flow actually work? What is this new Microsoft service doing under the hood? Why does this esoteric protocol that no one seems interested in behave the way it does? It’s not glamorous, and yeah nobody’s posting a sick demo video of themselves reading vendor docs on a Tuesday night in bed while their spouse watches Love Island. I get it. 

If you really want to pay the bills though and stand out from the rest of the pack in offsec, embrace this non-glamorous work. The really good operators understand the tech in the stack deep enough to know where their offsec-specific tradecraft applies, including the spots nobody else is looking at. This is where the magic happens. 

BONUS: you also double as a defensive SME for the tech at your company, making you materially more valuable.

items.author: ipurple

items.content:
The same architecture used by game hackers to bypass anti-cheat is the same architecture used by blue-pill malware to bypass EDRs. Understanding how to build one means understanding how to detect one too.

Two blue-pill hypervisors: UEFI boot-time kernel hooks, SSDT hooking, EPT page shadowing, execute-only shadow pages, dual-EPT context switching, Monitor Trap Flag single-stepping, PatchGuard bypass, memory integrity check bypass. All without modifying guest memory.

Custom hypervisors do not work when VBS is enabled unless you run on top of Hyper-V via nested virtualization. Game hackers often take the easier route and hide inside Hyper-V as an alternative, using its VM-exit handling and EPT/NPT. Building your own from scratch is harder but gives you full control and deeper understanding of the entire stack.

Once control is established, these techniques can be extended to conceal threads, processes, handles, and memory regions, or embed payloads like shellcode or reflective DLLs. All hidden from the Windows guest operating system and any security software. When the guest reads hooked memory, EPT returns the clean original page instead of the real malicious content. User-mode malware can communicate with the hypervisor via hypercalls to perform privileged actions on its behalf without ever touching the kernel.

Useful for malware analysis, reverse engineering, memory forensics, game security research, understanding EDR and anti-cheat evasion, and advanced adversary tradecraft at the hypervisor level.

Made by a secret club reverse engineer from New Zealand ("across-the-ditch").

Blog: https://t.co/FJwNFkYpzh
Illusion: https://t.co/sqvxPUwp1b
Matrix: https://t.co/z2iJ1TQPWm

Author: @memN0ps
Published on @the_secret_club 

#ReverseEngineering #WindowsInternals #Malware

items.author: _RastaMouse

items.content:
The discourse of AI coding today is just insane!

"Use LLMs for dev, it makes you 10x more productive"
"Avoid overcomplicating things, just prompt the LLM for your intended task and watch it go to work"
"But make sure you check the code, LLMs make mistakes"
"But don't check the code too much because that slows you down, you need to check just the right amount"
"Actually, just use a spec, a spec is now the agreed upon way to avoid shipping slop, you now shouldn't look at the code"
"Or actually just use plan mode, because a spec is too heavy for most projects"
"And introduce this grill-me skill, which will make sure you answer all questions that the LLM needs to build a PLAN.md file"
"Yeah you need guardrails to avoid the LLM spinning out of control, just add linting and formatting rules"
"And add in GitHub workflows to ensure that the PR the LLM makes doesn't break any build"
"Yes.. PR, you should be having your LLMs submit code as PRs which you review (but not too much reviewing remember) before merging"
"Yeah it shouldn't have access to the main branch, you need to enforce branch rules to disallow that"
"You're giving your LLM access to GitHub via a private access token? Dude you need a GitHub app and then you generate an installation token"
"OK, now we have that all in place, we scale up, work on 5 LLMs at the same time to increase your output"
"Worktrees are the answer to stopping each LLM from stepping over each other"
"What are you doing, since Fable you don't need any of that anymore, just prompt the model!"

Fucking exhausting!

items.author: Salsa12__

items.content:
RT @DailyDarkWeb: 🇨🇳 China - Alleged PLA Military Documents Offered for Sale on Underground Forum

A threat actor is advertising what are claimed to be Chinese People's Liberation Army (PLA) military documents for sale.

* The seller states that only "serious buyers" will be provided access to the file list and additional details.
* Multiple sample images were shared, appearing to show Chinese-language reports, testing documents, summaries, and technical materials.
* The post does not publicly disclose the volume of data, classification level, or the exact source of the alleged documents.
* The actor is accepting contact through multiple anonymous communication channels and claims escrow services are available.

Analyst Note: Claims involving military or government documents require a high degree of scrutiny, as underground forum posts frequently exaggerate, recycle previously leaked material, or misrepresent publicly available documents. However, if authentic, the exposure of military testing reports, operational assessments, or technical documentation could present significant intelligence and national security implications.

#DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #China
